Population: Published studies using structured or unstructured EHR data to identify social and behavioral determinants of health

One-Paragraph Summary: SDoH information was frequently incomplete, inconsistently standardized, and embedded in unstructured clinical notes. Title: Perceptions of hospital electronic health record (EHR) training, support, and patient safety by staff position and tenure
APA Authors: Campione, J., & Liu, H.
Design/Method: Cross-sectional secondary analysis of AHRQ SOPS Hospital Survey Version 1.0 and Health IT Patient Safety Supplemental Items; multivariable regression modeling
Framework: No explicit theoretical framework; supports patient safety culture and socio-technical alignment
Setting: 44 hospitals
Population: Hospital staff across clinical, management, and information technology roles
Sample/Scope: 8,880 staff; tenure analysis included 8,548 staff after excluding 332 information technology staff.
Data Source: 2017 AHRQ Surveys on Patient Safety Culture Hospital Survey Version 1.0 Database and Health IT Patient Safety Supplemental Items
Analysis: Regression modeling of training, support and communication, workflow, satisfaction, and patient safety/quality outcomes while controlling for hospital ownership type and bed size
One-Paragraph Summary: This multi-hospital study examined whether perceptions of EHR training, support, workflow, satisfaction, and patient safety differed by staff role and hospital tenure. Significant differences were found across professional groups and tenure categories, demonstrating that uniform EHR training and support may not address the needs of all users. The findings support targeted organizational investment informed by staff feedback.
Finding 1: Pharmacists reported significantly less favorable EHR training scores than registered nurses. Staff with less than one year of tenure reported lower training scores but more favorable support and communication than staff with 11 or more years of tenure. Physicians, hospital management, and information technology staff reported inaccurate EHR information more frequently than registered nurses. Hospitals were encouraged to use the AHRQ Health IT item set to identify perceptual incongruities and target EHR training and support.
Primary Themes: EHR training; organizational support; support and communication; workflow; patient safety; usability; satisfaction; staff position; tenure; targeted investment
Chapter 2 Use: Current Content; EHR Training; Organizational Support; Patient Safety; Workflow; STS; Research Gap
Framework Connection: No explicit framework; supports STS through evidence that EHR outcomes depend on the interaction of staff roles, tenure, workflow, training, support, and organizational conditions. Also supports ALT through the need for targeted, role-responsive training.
Phenomenon Connection: Directly supports exploration of how hospital staff, including registered nurses, perceive EHR training, support, workflow, and patient-safety implications. The role- and tenure-based variation reinforces the need for nurse-specific qualitative understanding of the transition from training to live documentation.
Study Limitations: Cross-sectional secondary analysis of 2017 self-report data; participating hospitals were voluntary and may not represent all U.S. hospitals; associations cannot establish causality; multidisciplinary sample was not nurse-specific.
Gap/Need: Supports role- and tenure-sensitive EHR training and organizational support, while leaving a need for qualitative exploration of nurses’ perceptions of how classroom-based preparation transfers to live clinical documentation.
Use Boundary: Use for organizational support, training, workflow, patient safety, and STS synthesis. Do not attribute all staff-group findings exclusively to nurses or state causal effects.

Title: Evolutionary game analysis of the longitudinal integration of electronic health record based on prospect theory
APA Authors: Hu, X., Wang, R., Wei, Y., Lin, H., & Gui, X.
Source Type: Quantitative evolutionary game simulation and prospect-theory modeling study
Design/Method: Quantitative theoretical modeling study using a three-party evolutionary game framework integrated with prospect theory and numerical simulation
Framework: Prospect theory and evolutionary game theory; supports socio-technical and organizational-readiness interpretation but does not formally test the dissertation’s selected STS framework
Setting: Vertical EHR integration within healthcare consortia and cross-institutional healthcare information-sharing environments
Population: Modeled stakeholder groups: healthcare institutions, patients, and local governments

Data Source: National policy documents, prior scholarly literature, theoretical parameter assumptions, and MATLAB-generated simulation trajectories

One-Paragraph Summary: The study modeled the longitudinal integration of electronic health records as a strategic interaction among healthcare institutions, patients, and government. Incorporating prospect theory into a three-party evolutionary game framework, the authors examined how initial willingness to participate, awareness and utilization of EHRs, healthcare-consortium cohesion, perceived gains and losses, and loss aversion shaped participation trajectories. Simulations indicated that stronger initial willingness, greater perceived record value, and closer interorganizational collaboration accelerated participation and stabilized EHR integration, while loss sensitivity, perceived costs, privacy and security concerns, and weak incentives could slow or destabilize engagement. The model also suggested that government involvement may shift from early facilitation toward later regulation and oversight as the integration system matures.
Finding 1: Greater initial willingness, EHR awareness and utilization, and healthcare-consortium cohesion increased active participation and accelerated stable EHR integration, whereas loss aversion, perceived costs, and gain-loss sensitivity produced more cautious or unstable participation patterns among healthcare institutions, patients, and government.

One-Paragraph Summary: Maxwell presented qualitative research design as an interactive system in which goals, the conceptual framework, research questions, methods, and validity strategies influence one another. He explained that conceptual frameworks may be developed from existing theory, prior research, experiential knowledge, pilot or exploratory studies, thought experiments, and concept maps. Concept mapping should emphasize the relationships among concepts, remain open to revision, and be accompanied by a narrative explaining what the map proposes about the phenomenon. For research questions asking how a process unfolds, a process map may be more suitable than a timeless variance map.

One-Paragraph Summary: Paas, Renkl, and Sweller explained that Cognitive Load Theory connects instructional design with human cognitive architecture. Working memory is limited when learners process novel information, whereas familiar information stored as schemas in long-term memory can be processed with substantially less working-memory demand. Skilled performance develops through the construction and automation of increasingly complex schemas. Instruction should therefore manage cognitive demands and organize information so learners can devote mental resources to schema acquisition rather than unnecessary processing.
Finding 1: Learning is supported when instructional conditions align with human cognitive architecture. Working memory is limited when processing novel information, familiar schemas reduce working-memory demand, and skilled performance develops through schema construction and automation.

Title: Case study research: Design and methods (4th ed.)
APA Authors: Yin, R. K.
Design/Method: Methodological text on case study design, evidence collection, analysis, reporting, validity, reliability, and case study protocol development
Framework: Case study logic emphasizing bounded inquiry, multiple sources of evidence, triangulation, a case study database, and a maintained chain of evidence

Analysis: Methodological synthesis and procedural guidance for designing and conducting rigorous case studies
One-Paragraph Summary: Yin identified six common sources of case study evidence: documents, archival records, interviews, direct observation, participant-observation, and physical artifacts. He emphasized three principles for rigorous case study data collection: use multiple sources of evidence that converge on the same facts or findings, create a case study database distinct from the final report, and maintain a chain of evidence linking research questions, protocol procedures, evidence, and conclusions. These practices support construct validity, reliability, and transparent interpretation of a contemporary phenomenon within its context.
Finding 1: Case study evidence may include documents, archival records, interviews, direct observation, participant-observation, and physical artifacts. Multiple sources should converge on the same facts or findings; a formal case study database should remain distinct from the final report; and a chain of evidence should link research questions, protocol procedures, collected evidence, and conclusions. These practices strengthen construct validity and reliability.
